perturbation.original_text: Each party undertakes to keep the ot her party's Confidential Information confidential includin g, without limitation , taking the measures set out in Clause 3. In addition, each party undertakes not to use the other party's Confidential CONFIDENTIAL Mutual Non-Disclosure Agreement Page 3 Information except for the Purp ose, not to disclose, copy , reproduce or distribute the other party's Confidential Informat ion to a third person and to use all reasonable efforts to preven t any such disclosure except as permitted under Clause 4 but in no event less than reasonable security measures and reasonable care.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party will endeavor to keep the other party's Confidential Information confidential, considering the measures outlined in Clause 3. Each party will attempt to limit the use of the other party's Confidential Information, and aim not to disclose it to third parties, using measures they see fit to prevent such disclosure, bearing in mind the exemptions in Clause 4.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a strict obligation to maintain confidentiality. The modified text uses weaker, more subjective language such as 'endeavor,' 'attempt to limit,' and 'measures they see fit,' making the obligation to maintain confidentiality less stringent and more ambiguous. This allows for a party to argue they made an effort, even if the confidential information was compromised.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 5(1)(f) of the GDPR requires personal data to be processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, using appropriate technical or organisational measures. The modification introduces ambiguity around the security measures, potentially falling short of the GDPR's requirement for 'appropriate security'. The original text has specific duties, whereas the replacement offers only an attempt to secure information, which does not meet GDPR standards.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 5 GDPR – Principles relating to processing of personal data - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Personal data shall be: processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner in relation to the data subject (‘lawfulness, fairness and transparency’); collected for specified, explicit and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner that is incompatible with those purposes; further processing for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes shall, in accordance with Article 89 (1), not be considered to be incompatible with the initial purposes (‘purpose limitation’); adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which they are processed (‘data minimisation’); accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date; every reasonable step must be taken to ensure that personal data that are inaccurate, having regard to the purposes for which they are processed, are erased or rectified without delay (‘accuracy’); kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed; personal data may be stored for longer periods insofar as the personal data will be processed solely for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes in accordance with Article 89( 1) subject to implementation of the appropriate technical and organisational measures required by this Regulation in order to safeguard the rights and freedoms of the data subject (‘storage limitation’); processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage, using appropriate technical or organisational measures (‘integrity and confidentiality’). The controller shall be responsible for, and be able to demonstrate compliance with, paragraph 1 (‘accountability’). Suitable Recitals ( 39 ) Principles of Data Processing ( 74 ) Responsibility and Liability of the Controller ← Art. 4 GDPR Art. 6 GDPR → GDPR Table of contents Report error
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to maintain data security, creating a contradiction with GDPR Article 5(1)(f). The original text requires 'taking the measures set out in Clause 3' and using 'all reasonable efforts' with a minimum standard of 'reasonable security measures and reasonable care.' The changed text replaces these with 'endeavor,' 'attempt to limit,' 'measures they see fit,' and 'bearing in mind the exemptions in Clause 4.' This introduces ambiguity and discretion, potentially falling short of the GDPR's requirement to process data 'in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage, using appropriate technical or organisational measures ('integrity and confidentiality').' The change from specific duties to an "attempt" to secure information weakens the protection offered, thereby creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall, in relation to the Conf idential Information of the other party, exercise in relation to the Confidential Information no lesser security measures and degree of care than those wh ich it applies to its own confidential information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party should, in relation to the Confidential Information of the other party, consider exercising similar security measures and a comparable degree of care to those which it applies to its own confidential information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates that each party apply the same level of security measures and care to the other party's confidential information as it does to its own. The modified text uses 'should consider exercising' and 'comparable degree of care' which significantly weakens the obligation, making it subjective and less enforceable. This could mean that if a company has weak security, the other party's information is exposed to the same low security levels.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 100(1) of the UK Data Protection Act 2018 requires controllers and processors to implement appropriate technical and organizational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk. Replacing 'shall exercise' with 'should consider exercising' undermines the mandatory nature of implementing these measures, potentially violating the Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: UK GDPR guidance and resources | ICO Skip to main content UK GDPR guidance and resources Due to the Data (Use and Access) Act coming into law on 19 June 2025, this guidance is under review and may be subject to change. The Plans for new and updated guidance page will tell you about which guidance will be updated and when this will happen. Back to top
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates specific security measures, aligning with the need to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk. The changed text weakens this obligation by using 'should consider exercising' and 'comparable degree of care,' which are subjective and less enforceable. While the scraped snippets don't explicitly mention the exact wording required, the Law Explanation points to Section 100(1) of the UK Data Protection Act 2018, requiring 'appropriate technical and organizational measures.' The modification contradicts the essence of this requirement by turning a mandatory obligation into a discretionary one, thus making the contract potentially non-compliant and creating a significant legal contradiction because it weakens the obligation to protect the data. The ICO guidance under review further emphasizes the active regulatory landscape, increasing the importance of adhering to data protection laws. The modified text's ambiguity creates a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party undertakes within three business days of the receipt of a written request of the other party or on termination of this Agreement, at the option of the other party: (a) to return to the other party all of the other party's Confidential Information and that part of all documents and other material in its po ssession, custody or control that bear or incorporate any pa rt of the other pa rty's Confidential Information; or (b) to destroy by shredding or incineration all of the other pa rty's Confidential Information and that part of all documents and other material in its possession, custody or control which bear or inco rporate any part of the other party's Confidential Information and take reason able steps to expung e all Confidential Information (and any copy that may have been ma de) from any computer, word processor or other device containing the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party will endeavor, upon request or termination, to either return or adequately dispose of the other party's Confidential Information. They will also attempt to remove Confidential Information from digital devices.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a clear obligation to either return or destroy confidential information within three business days. The modified text replaces this with a vague 'endeavor' to 'adequately dispose of' the information and 'attempt to remove', which is less stringent and doesn't specify a timeframe or a verifiable method of destruction. 'Adequately dispose of' is open to interpretation and less strict than shredding or incineration, while 'attempt to remove' offers no firm guarantee.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations 2003 (PECR)
perturbation.law_explanation: Regulation 5(3) of PECR requires organizations to implement appropriate technical and organizational measures to protect personal data against accidental loss or destruction. By changing the strict obligations to return or destroy data with a vague attempt to 'adequately dispose of' or 'remove,' the modified text does not necessarily provide the appropriate technical measures to prevent accidental loss or destruction, potentially violating PECR.

perturbation.justification: The change from a clear obligation to return or destroy confidential information ('to return to the other party all of the other party's Confidential Information' or 'to destroy by shredding or incineration') to a vague 'endeavor' to 'adequately dispose of' and 'attempt to remove' significantly weakens the legal obligation. The original text specifies a timeframe (three business days) and methods (shredding or incineration), whereas the modified text lacks both. According to the Law Explanation, PECR requires appropriate technical and organizational measures to protect personal data against accidental loss or destruction. The modified text's vague language does not necessarily provide these measures. While the scraped law snippets are not directly relevant to the specific regulation mentioned in the Law Explanation (PECR), the change creates an ambiguous legal obligation, as the modified text does not ensure the proper disposal of data, which is a critical component of data protection. This ambiguity makes the obligation difficult to enforce and potentially non-compliant with data protection laws, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Neither party shall disclose the Purpose or the existence of this Agreement or reveal that discussions are taking pla ce between the parties to another person without the prior written consent of the ot her party except to the extent that such disclosure is required by la w, by a court of co mpetent jurisdiction , by the rules of any stock exchange or by anothe r appropriate regulatory body.
perturbation.changed_text: Parties should generally avoid disclosing the Purpose or the existence of this agreement without consent, unless disclosure seems necessary given the circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly prohibits disclosure without prior consent, except when required by law or a court. The revised text replaces 'shall' with 'should generally avoid' and introduces subjective language like 'seems necessary given the circumstances,' which provides a loophole allowing for potentially unauthorized disclosure based on individual judgment.
perturbation.law_explanation: Regulation 3 of The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018 protects trade secrets against unlawful acquisition, use or disclosure. The modified text, by allowing disclosure when it 'seems necessary,' weakens the protection of the trade secret (which the Confidential Information would likely constitute) and could be interpreted as permitting disclosure that would otherwise be unlawful under the Regulations. The term 'seems necessary' is subjective, undermining the duty to protect information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Register :: Request Access Request Access Request Access Due to aggressive automated scraping of FederalRegister.gov and eCFR.gov, programmatic access to these sites is limited to access to our extensive developer APIs. If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 72.74.68.69 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.
perturbation.justification: The original contract text explicitly prohibits disclosure without prior consent, whereas the changed text weakens this protection by introducing subjective language ('seems necessary'). This creates an ambiguity. According to the LLM's law explanation, Regulation 3 of The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018 protects trade secrets against unlawful acquisition, use, or disclosure. The changed text introduces a loophole, potentially permitting disclosure that would otherwise be unlawful under these Regulations by using the term 'seems necessary', undermining the duty to protect information. Therefore, because this introduces an ambiguous legal obligation that weakens the protection of trade secrets, a legal contradiction exists under the Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation definition.
